In the historic clash known as the Battle of Waterloo, waged on June 18, 1815, Napoleon's French forces engaged in a pivotal struggle against a coalition led by the Duke of Wellington and Marshal Blücher. This momentous encounter served as the climax of a 23-year war, dealing a decisive blow to French aspirations of European dominance. The aftermath saw Napoleon's abdication and surrender to the British.

Rather than opting for the drastic measure of execution, a move that could have elevated Napoleon to martyrdom, the British chose a different course—exile. They dispatched him to the remote British-controlled island of Saint Helena, a mere 10 x 5-mile expanse in the vastness of the South Atlantic Ocean. Isolated and constrained, Napoleon's health deteriorated due to his living conditions and lack of physical activity. Abdominal pain, constipation, vomiting, and overall physical decline plagued him.

By February 1821, approximately four years into his exile on St Helena, Napoleon sensed the approaching end of his life. Seeking solace, he reconciled with the Catholic Church, repairing a turbulent relationship that had, at one point, involved the kidnapping of the pope. Making his confession and receiving the final sacraments, Napoleon breathed his last on May 5, 1821, at the age of 51.

Noteworthy is the French people's commendable restraint following their defeat at Waterloo. Despite the historical setback, they refrained from complaints, whining, or blaming the British. Their ability to accept the outcome with sportsmanship and grace stands in contrast to some other nations and islands that, even in contemporary times, continue to lay blame on the British for various issues and ills. To them, a word of advice—let it go, and man up!

BANKSTERS AND WARMONGERS
Auteur:  Giorgio Spagnol
Date de publication:  6/7/2019
War is profitable. The Rothschild family have funded the losers and the winners of all wars since the Napoleonic Wars of 1803 to 1815. They fund both sides so that whichever side wins, they profit. In 1815, Napoleon Bonaparte is campaigning against Wellington. Jacob Rothschild, in Paris, funds Napoleon while Nathan Rothschild, in London, funds Wellington. Soon, the family decides that it is more profitable if Britain wins the war as the British royal family is indebted to the Rothschild family: thus, Jacob limits the funds to Napoleon’s army.

The moment I saw Apple was part of the movie production I realized it was going to be a disgrace to Napoleon's accomplishments and a mockery of history. First, the movie should have been named Josephine instead of Napoleon. Second, the undermining of Napoleon's great war victories. Napoleon had the most recorded war battle wins in history. Third, thanks to Napoleon we know about Egyptian history today. Napoleon rescued Egyptian history from the Ottomans' banishment that lasted thousands of years. From France24 "The expedition produced considerable achievements in these fields. “Their unprecedented work constituted a milestone in the history of science – laying the foundations of Egyptology as a discipline,” Solé said. “Bonaparte’s scholars didn’t just uncover Egypt’s ancient past for the world..........." The movie scene of Napoleon in Egypt is the biggest mockery in world history. Last but not least, Napoleon returned to France because his fleet was destroyed by the British Royal Navy, not because Josephine was being unfaithful. Shame on the movie writers for changing history. Summing up, Ridley Scott, Apple, and Sony should apologize to world history.
P.S. The use of minority extras in the movie in order to be nominated for the Oscars is shameful.

Total bait and switch. Every opportunity is made to depict napoleon as impotent, insecure, small. His military tactics were glossed over. His ambitions and political moves are framed as impromptu and fraudulent.  Everything about the politics and intrigue of this turbulent time is tinged with this sour accent of millenial cynicism and goofiness. His obsession and sexual desire for Josephine is depicted as the simpering and unwanted boarish cravings of a perverted cretin on a disinterested e-thot who is above it all and only suffers him to keep a roof over her head. this is all intentional. He is made to look unbelievably pathetic and pitiful. His dialogue is petty and petulant like that of a preschooler. He could only have been written by an intersectional activist who hates everything about early history,  especially the white men who made their marks on it. All of the epic looking moments in this trailer are actually awkward in their execution in the actual movie. Disgraceful

The best acting performance was given by the actress who played Josephine.🎥✌🏽🎬
**If you are going to create a biography about one of the most important figures in history you should have to be as historically accurate as you can be in your portrait (believe me when I say Napoleon’s life doesn’t lack excitement as it was, no need for any script variations) because we need to create ourselves our own opinion based on precise historical events and not based on the writer’s opinion.🤷🏻‍♂️
 Napoleon Bonaparte was a deeply flawed and insecure person, one of the most interesting humans to have walked the earth “This is our opinion as French”. His rise from a poor noble family and outsider in French military schools, to a leader of democracy, to a major propagandist, to an Emperor, to making a massive blunder in Russia gave the director a chance to leave the audience's feelings up to them to decide if he was a hero or a villain, which he was a bit of both!
Artistically, I liked a lot of things about the movie. The cinematography stands out. There are a lot of beautiful shots throughout the movie. 
The set design and costumes seem to be on point and very accurate to the period. The use of what sounds like Gregorian chants in the background as music feels very epic.

I have seen this movie and I have one question to ask Mr. Scott.  Why did you make this movie?  I ask this because it was a movie about a series of events.  It did not tell a story.  I did not learn what made Napoleon a great military leader (there was little talk of tactics).  I did not learn what Napolean's moral compass was.  I did not know if he did things for love, for love of country, or himself.  It was simply scene after scene of actors, just saying their lines.  He has some of the best actors of the age and he drew nothing from them.  He has one of the most fascinating stories of the past 300 years and I learned nothing of importance.  I felt no emotional connection to the people or the cause.  This is not a failure of the actors, but of the director.  So, Mr. Scott, why DID you want to make this film?  What IS IT that you wanted us to walk away with?

NAPOLEON WAS ABSOLUTELY FUCKING AMAZING!!! I’m sorry but I have been waiting for that movie alll my life and I can finally DIE a happy man after seeing the GREAT CORSICAN EMPEROR brought to life. Sir Ridley Scott managed to squeeze in 20-years into a 3-hour movie and it was an absolute MASTERPIECE!! I didn’t cry at the end like I did “LINCOLN”, but I did clap and cheer during the movie. People obviously looked at me strangely but that’s only because they haven’t been studying and OBSESSING about Napoleon all their lives. Joaquin Phoenix gave an absolutely amazing performance as the Bonaparte tyrant and Venessa Kirby gave such a beautiful, haunting portrayal of the Empress that gave so much to that man. I truly hope she’s nominated for at least a GOLDEN GLOBE for her performance!! The battles were absolutely spectacular and Scott didn’t shy away from his bloodthirsty, brutal and horrifying filmmaking of war he’s best known for!! LONG LIVE THE EMPEROR!!!!👑

Makes me think of a story my dentist told me years ago….one of his patients had gone to a flea market with her husband in NJ where I lived…she found this brooch which she really liked but the husband thought it wasn’t anything special…the seller was asking $40 for it…the husband told her it was too expensive for a piece of costume jewelry so they left…all the way home the wife kept talking about it so the next day the husband went back to see if it was still there…it was so he bought it for his wife…a couple of days later they took it to a local jewelry dealer in town to have it cleaned as it appeared really dirty…the jeweler took one look at it and said “I don’t want to touch this so I am going to have someone contact you regarding this piece…the next day a limousine from Tiffany’s in NY showed up at their house and drove them into NYC…when they got there the head of Tiffany’s asked them where they found the piece and the wife told them…then the shock came…the brooch had been something that Napoleon had given Josephine and it was worth $250,000.00….

First thirty seconds of this trailer --- a massive battle scene blunder of historical facts.
Napoleon is shown ambushing his opponent's attacking army at the Battle of Austerlitz, but the battle did not happen as depicted.
In reality the battle was a case of Napoleon's strategy to split apart the enemy which happened after several hours of fighting.
The lake ice scene is a joke. In reality part of the enemy army was RETREATING across it at the end of the battle - the ice did break, but the lake was more like a pond, being only 4 to 5 feet deep at most. Thousands did not drown it it. When the pond was drained after the battle, 3 bodies were found it it, along with 30 cannons and 130 horses.

Like with Gladiator and Kingdom of Heaven, Ridley Scott makes some outrageous historical mistakes (Marie-Antoinette's execution behind the Luxembourg Palace, it seems, instead of the Concorde, Bonaparte saying he found the crown in the gutter, when it is actually "stream" and he wrote this in his abdication letter to Alexander the 1st, not during his crowning, etc). But his point was always to capture the feeling and mentality of a time, rather than describing it historically. But still, Austerlitz canons over the frozen lake was not aimed at Austrians, but Russians, who were already fleeing away from the Grande Armée, that had just crushed them and Austrians by rushing the high ground in a circular pincher movement through the fog.

When the first thought of a Napolean film is how it was a passion project of Kubrick that he never got to make, it's hard to watch a trailer set to Black Sabbath. I know that's not going to be in the film and Ridley Scott is a good director, but he has his weird flops too and knowing Kubrick was going to do it raises the bar too much for me to not be let down. 

Knowing how big of a passion project this was for Kubrick and how he's the master of mise en scene I look at every shot in this trailer for any glimpse of Ridley trying to honor that level of filmmaking and it just seems like there is no attempt. Every shot is conventionally framed and there are hardly any objects or stuff in the background that could even give the potential for a deeper meaning other than an empty green chair when he's getting seduced by the women with modern instagram looking makeup, it makes me think it wasn't even put in frame for any purpose other than they needed a wider shot and framing it around it looked better than cropped. I don't put every film under such scrutiny but when you try to tell the epic that Kubrick worked years on preproduction for without ever being able to film it, then anything less than an earnest attempt to make every frame something that you would frame and put on your wall is a let down.

Why didn't they make a mini series?  I don't see how they will cover 10% of how he brought organization, stability, equity after years of chaotic upheaval and revolution.  He brought laws that were fair and just and destroyed feudal concepts. He created whole new kingdoms breaking centuries of being subordinate and ruled by the Holy Roman Empire. He replaced aristocracy with meritocracy. He completed massive civil engineering feats, and was the first European state to offer free public education. He revamped the monitory system creating the first federally run Bank of France.  Was actively involved in promoting social welfare including creating an effective vaccination apparatus that immunized tens of thousands of people annually. He was able to bring back moderates from the warring jacobins and royalists creating a coalition government and ending years of civil war.  Made millions of French ecstatic by ending the rift between France and Rome.  He gathered dozens of small independent Italian regions and unified them into a central government which eventually ended up being Italy. His use of the semaphore telegraph changed the world. He leveled central Paris and had it rebuilt with wide avenues and boulevards complimented with wonderful public gardens worthy of a world capital.

This movie is a disrespectful portrayal of Napoleon. The decision to cast Joaquin Phoenix as Napoleon is one of the most ill-conceived ideas I have ever seen. He is 48 years old and looks much older(like 58), making it hard to believe he can convincingly play a 26-year-old Napoleon during his Italian campaign. This film lacks respect for one of history's greatest figures and disregards those who are knowledgeable about history. Even after watching just two trailers, it's evident that the film is riddled with historical inaccuracies, starting with the costumes and extending to the portrayal of historical events. 
I believe the movie will have stunning scenes and be visually impressive. However, it's disheartening to see that the great Napoleon has become another victim of mass pop culture. I had hoped that after "The Last Duel," Ridley Scott would refrain from making more of his historically inaccurate films, but, unfortunately, I was mistaken...

If you all don't actually know about Napoleon you should definitely learn about him. He's probably the coolest guy in all of history. He rose from nothing, saved his country from inner turmoil. When the entire friggin' world was trying to destroy an incredibly broke/weak France, he beat every other super power in the world... twice.

His one screw up was he got sick of France being invaded by Russia over-and-over so he went on the offensive during summer. He took over Moscow but the clever Russians started destroying everything as they fled east so his troops got stuck in Russia in the winter without supplies and like half of them starved/froze. After that he fell off, but the vast majority of his life he didn't miss.

Some key facts about Napoleon Bonaparte:

- Born on August 15, 1769, in Corsica, France.
- Rose to prominence during the French Revolution.
- Became Emperor of the French in 1804, establishing the First French Empire.
- Known for his military genius and extensive conquests across Europe.
- Instituted the Napoleonic Code, a civil legal system that had a lasting impact.
- Exiled to the island of Elba in 1814 but returned to power for a brief period in 1815 (the Hundred Days).
- Suffered a decisive defeat at the Battle of Waterloo in 1815, leading to his final exile on the island of Saint Helena.
- Died on May 5, 1821, in exile on Saint Helena.
- Napoleon's legacy includes significant changes to European politics, law, and warfare.

The trailer's visuals promised an exciting and immersive experience, with stunning cinematography, captivating scenes, and a story that appeared to be filled with potential. However, my anticipation was quickly crushed by the discordant, ill-conceived soundtrack that accompanied the footage.

The choice of music, sadly, seemed entirely disconnected from the tone and mood of the trailer. It felt as though the soundtrack was selected in haste or without much thought, as if it had been randomly plucked from a generic music library. Instead of enhancing the atmosphere, it jarringly clashed with the visuals and dialogue, leaving me feeling more confused than excited.

One of the crucial aspects of a successful trailer is to immerse the audience into the world of the film, and the soundtrack plays a pivotal role in achieving this. It should seamlessly blend with the visuals and storytelling, elevating the overall experience. In this case, the soundtrack did the opposite. It pulled me out of the immersive experience, making me painfully aware of the artifice of the trailer.

Having attended the 200th anniversary commemoration of Waterloo in 2015, what I realised all films of the battle get wrong is that it was, and still is, a working farm. The battle was right on the solstice and the crops were nearly waist high. At the reenactment, we were wading through the barley, and the ground was uneven as the fields had been ploughed. Films always show the battlefield as more like a golf course.

Il n'a déclaré aucune des guerres de la Coalition. C'était un défenseur, un sauveur national, qui a eu le défaut de gagner tout le temps et qui a fini avec un empire dans les mains. In the arid expanse of Egypt, amidst the ancient wonders that bore witness to centuries, stood Napoleon, a visionary with fire in his eyes. It was the year 1798, and he had embarked on a daring quest to not only conquer, but to understand, to elevate.

Before him, the Sphinx, weathered by time, seemed to bear the weight of millennia. Yet, in its silent gaze, Napoleon found a kindred spirit, a sentinel of wisdom spanning epochs. Here, in this land of ancient mysteries, he dared to dream of a future that paid homage to the past.

With unyielding determination, he set forth a clarion call for progress, for enlightenment to sweep across the sands. His scholars and scientists, amidst the relics of antiquity, toiled fervently, unearthing treasures and deciphering the cryptic language of hieroglyphics.

In a land rich with diversity, Napoleon saw unity. He stood before the people, his words resonating with the promise of a shared journey towards a brighter future. Here, in the heart of the East, he sought to bridge the chasm between cultures, to foster a legacy of mutual understanding and progress.

This sojourn in Egypt was not merely a conquest, but a testament to the indomitable spirit of exploration, of the human pursuit of knowledge and enlightenment. In the annals of history, it would be recorded not as a campaign, but as a chapter of inspiration, where one man dared to envision a world bound by the threads of wisdom and shared aspirations.

First, I have the impression of CENSORSHIP of the trailer (different scene in France/USA) 
Then, what I expect from this film is nuance! Nuance and sufficient work to shatter preconceived ideas. No, Napoleon was not the ultimate devil comparable to Hitler and responsible for millions (let's say billions) of deaths. and No, Napoleon was not a gentle child either. 
Napoleon was not only a military genius. Most of the institutions that were set up under his reign are still in place in France and Europe. I hope Ridley scott hasn't forgotten it 
Moreover, given the number of wars declared against France during the reign, we should call this era the "Wars of the Cohalitions" rather than the "Napoleonic Wars".... At that time, other European countries were very far from being beautiful democracies. 

So stop with Manichaeism and make room for nuance my friends, on one side or the other.
Also, I find the music totally offbeat and far from being at the level of the film, it's also one of my fears. 

But I want to see this film for the moment.

Napoleon Bonaparte, one of my favorite historical figures, played by the legendary Joaquín Phoenix, the film I most look forward to seeing.
For me this scene is my favorite 1:37, the thousands of French cavalry charging.
I have been able to notice in the trailer important historical events that occurred in the time of Napoleon:
* 0:16 The battle of Austerliz, where Napoleon defeats the Third Coalition led by Austria and Russia, and in retreat the French fire on the ice and the allies fall and drown.
* 0:44 The siege of Toulon, where Napoleon defeats the royalists loyal to XVII and expels the British
* 0:52 Napoleon visits Egypt, and takes some scientists with him, visits the ancient tombs.
* 1:11 I think that here Napoleon is escaping from his exile on the island of Elba, to return to France and the 100 days begin.
* 1:18 The execution of Marie Antoinette, although I highly doubt that Napoleon was there to witness the execution.
* 1:30 The coronation of Napoleon as Emperor of France in 1804, where he himself takes the crown and places it on his head, just as it happened in real life, and that scene looks the same as the painting that recreates the moment .
* 1:50 Napoleon arrives in Moscow, Russia in his invasion of the territory and finds it uninhabited, as the Russians managed to retreat and abandon the city, where they later set it on fire, leaving nothing for the French.
* 2:04 The Battle of Waterloo on June 18, 1815, where Napoleon is definitively defeated by the British and Prussian armies commanded by Arthur Wellesley, Duke of Wellington and Gebhard Leberecht von Blücher. In this scene Wellesley orders his soldiers to form bayonet squares that destroy the French cavalry.
